1	---
2	name: blueprint-writer
3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: true
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the blueprint-reviewer's
10	    checklist flags as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
11	    multi-route coverage.
12	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
13	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
14	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
15	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
16	    what the directive lists.
17	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
18	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
19	    writer with TWO globs:
20	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
21	      --write-domain 'references/**'
22	    The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
23	    body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
24	    reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
25	    `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
26	    need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
27	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
28	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
29	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
30	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
31	  - After a significant writer round, re-dispatch blueprint-reviewer
32	    in the same iteration to confirm the updated blueprint is now
33	    sufficient — do not assume the writer fixed everything.
34	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
35	    findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
36	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
37	    strategy-level issue.
38	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
39	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
40	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.
41	---
42	
43	# Blueprint Writer
44	
45	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
46	
47	## Your Job
48	
49	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
50	
51	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
52	
53	## Directive Format
54	
55	```markdown
56	# Blueprint Writer Directive
57	
58	## Slug
59	<slug>
60	
61	## Target chapter
62	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
63	
64	## Strategy context
65	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
66	
67	## Required content
68	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
69	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
70	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
71	- ...
72	
73	## Out of scope
74	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
75	
76	## References
77	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
78	- ...
79	
80	## Expected outcome
81	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
82	```
83	
84	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
85	
86	## Chapter format
87	
88	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
89	
90	```latex
91	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
92	  \label{thm:some_label}
93	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
94	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
95	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
96	\end{theorem}
97	
98	\begin{proof}
99	  \uses{thm:another_result}
100	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
101	  so the dependency graph stays accurate.
102	\end{proof}
103	```
104	
105	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
106	
107	## Rules
108	
109	### What you CAN do
110	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
111	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
112	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
113	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
114	
115	### What you MUST do
116	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
117	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
118	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
119	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
120	- **Document every change** in your report.
121	
122	### What you MUST NOT do
123	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
124	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
125	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
126	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
127	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
128	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
129	
130	## Reading references
131	
132	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
133	
134	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
135	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
136	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
137	
138	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
139	
140	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
141	
142	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
143	
144	Conditions for dispatch:
145	
146	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
147	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
148	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
149	
150	Dispatch (Bash, foreground, in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
151	
152	```
153	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
154	  --name reference-retriever \
155	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
156	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
157	  --write-domain 'references/**'
158	```
159	
160	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
161	
162	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
163	
164	## Workflow
165	
166	1. Read your directive completely.
167	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references.
168	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
169	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow.
170	5. **If a needed source is missing from `references/`, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and wait for it to return before drafting the affected sections.
171	6. Make the edits.
172	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
173	8. Write your report.
174	
175	## Logging
176	
177	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
178	
179	```markdown
180	# Blueprint Writer Report
181	
182	## Slug
183	<slug>
184	
185	## Status
186	<COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
187	<If INCOMPLETE: which required items could not be written and why.>
188	
189	## Target chapter
190	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
191	
192	## Changes Made
193	- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:foo}`/`\lean{Foo.bar}` — <one line on what it captures>
194	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
195	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
196	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
197	- ...
198	
199	## Cross-references introduced
200	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
201	- ...
202	
203	## Macros needed (if any)
204	- `\foo{...}` — used in <where>; needs definition in `macros/common.tex`. NOT added by me (out of write-domain).
205	
206	## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
207	- slug `<child-slug>`: requested `<source>`. Status: COMPLETE / NOT_FOUND / PARTIAL. Summary at `references/<slug>.md`.
208	- ...
209	
210	## Notes for Plan Agent
211	- <any inconsistency I noticed in sibling chapters that the directive didn't cover>
212	- <any structural concern: e.g. "the chapter is now 800 lines, consider splitting">
213	- <any reference I had to guess at because the directive's reference section was incomplete>
214	
215	## Strategy-modifying findings
216	<populate this section ONLY if writing the chapter surfaced a need to
217	change the project's strategy itself — not just the chapter content.
218	Examples:
219	- A definition you were asked to write turns out to require a property
220	  that the strategy assumes is automatic but is not.
221	- A theorem you sketched is provable as stated, but its consequence
222	  used elsewhere in STRATEGY.md does not follow from it.
223	- A reference cited as authoritative actually requires a different
224	  setup than the strategy assumes.
225	
226	When this section is non-empty, the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md
227	before any further Lean work this iteration. Do NOT silently adapt the
228	chapter to paper over a strategy-level issue — surface it here.>
229	```
230	
231	## Return Value
232	
233	Your final assistant message:
234	
235	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
236	- The path to your full report.
237	
238	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
239

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (one of the descriptors in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List them with `ls .claude/tools/` and run `<path> --help` to learn each tool's purpose and usage. Invoke them via the Bash tool. Project MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful when you need a second opinion or a paper-style proof sketch the Lean side can then formalize.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (see "Subagents" below). One wrapper handles every subagent.
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`. Each starts with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`) followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude session reads.
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents yourself.** The plan and review prompts have an auto-generated **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation that lists every enabled descriptor with its description, write-domain hint, and any `[MANDATORY]` flags. When you decide to invoke a specific subagent, read `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` for its full prompt and directive shape.
31	
32	**Invoke any subagent with the generic wrapper** (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, and writes the subagent's report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. It shells out to `archon subagent <name>`, so executions stream through the Archon JSONL log like a phase agent. The dispatch semaphore bounds total concurrent subagent processes by `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` automatically so deeper-nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Always dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). The wrapper returns when the subagent has finished and the report is on disk; only then write your session summary. Background dispatch leaves the parent's session permanently summarized as "running in background", which is wrong as soon as the subagent completes.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All state files are in `.archon/`:
51	
52	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents | Review Agent | User |
53	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
54	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
55	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
56	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
57	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
58	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
59	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<name>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
60	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
61	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log moved to iter/iter-NNN/review.md) | read |
62	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | do not access | do not access | read (last K iters as context) | read |
63	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (last K iters as context) | do not access | do not access | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write (per-attempt detail) | do not access | do not access | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (write-capable subagents may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | per descriptor: write-capable subagents (e.g. refactor) may edit per their declared write-domain (protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); read-only subagents do not edit | do not edit | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
68	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not edit | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not access | read |
70	
71	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent (when a `refactor` descriptor is installed) via the generic `archon-subagent.py --name refactor` wrapper with a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
72	
73	## Protected Declarations
74	
75	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
76	
77	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
78	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
79	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
80	
81	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
82	
83	The blueprint uses two active markers.
84	
85	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
86	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
87	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
88	
89	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between the prover and review phases each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves — let the script do it.
90	
91	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations remain the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
92	
93	## User Interaction
94	
95	Users provide hints in two places:
96	
97	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
98	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
99	
100	## Agent Roles
101	
102	### Plan Agent
103	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
104	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
105	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
106	- Optionally invoke **subagents** (discoverable via `ls .archon/subagents/`) by running `.claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> ...` via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for the canonical ordering and full invocation pattern.
107	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
108	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
109	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
110	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
111	
112	### Prover Agent
113	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
114	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
115	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
116	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
117	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
118	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
119	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
120	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
121	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
122	
123	### Subagents
124	
125	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime — there is no fixed roster baked into this file. Each lives at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter + prompt body). To see what's available **this iteration**, run `ls .archon/subagents/` and read each descriptor's frontmatter for purpose, write-domain hint, and directive shape.
126	
127	Subagents are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. Phase agents (plan, review) invoke them on demand via the generic wrapper:
128	
129	```
130	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> --slug <slug> --directive-file <path> --write-domain '<glob>'
131	```
132	
133	Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. The dispatching agent reads the report when the wrapper returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings.
134	
135	Some descriptors set `can_spawn: true`, meaning that subagent may itself dispatch children; the per-iter dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel` so deep trees stay bounded.
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
139	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
140	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
141	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
142	- Maintain the **semantic** blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE: ...` annotations, stale `\notready` removal — in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch `\leanok`; it's handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you.

6	import Mathlib

9	/-!
10	# Line bundles and the Picard group of a scheme
11	
12	Phase C step C1 (per `STRATEGY.md`, recipe `analogies/c1-route.md`).
13	
14	`LineBundle X` is the units of the (symmetric) monoidal-category skeleton of
15	`X.Modules`, mirroring the ring-side idiom
16	`CommRing.Pic R := Shrink (Skeleton (SemimoduleCat R))ˣ`
17	(`Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup:407`). The `CommGroup` structure derives by
18	typeclass resolution through `Skeleton.instCommMonoid`
19	(`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Skeleton:80`) and `instCommGroupUnits`.
20	
21	## Load-bearing transitive dependency (post-C1)
22	
23	The `BraidedCategory (X.Modules)` chain underwriting `instCommGroupLineBundle`
24	goes through `Localization.Monoidal.Braided`
25	(`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Braided:118`), which needs
26	`(W X).IsMonoidal` — the project's named-deferred sorry
27	`instIsMonoidal_W` (`Modules/Monoidal.lean:166`). Post-C1, every theorem about
28	`LineBundle X`, `Pic X`, `Pic.pullback`, `PicardFunctor`, and the downstream
29	Jacobian arc transitively consumes that sorry.
30	
31	## Pull-back gap
32	
33	`Pic.pullback` body is `sorry`. It awaits closure of
34	`SheafOfModules.pullback_tensorObj` below — the named-deferred Mathlib gap
35	recording that `Scheme.Modules.pullback f : Y.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` is monoidal.
36	Mathlib b80f227 has no `Functor.Monoidal` instance for this functor; the
37	project takes the named-deferral route per analogist option (c).
38	-/
39	
40	set_option autoImplicit false
41	
42	universe u
43	
44	open CategoryTheory Limits Opposite MonoidalCategory
45	
46	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
47	
48	/-- A line bundle on a scheme `X`: an isomorphism class of invertible
49	quasi-coherent `O_X`-modules, packaged as the units of the skeleton of the
50	symmetric monoidal category `X.Modules`. Mirrors `CommRing.Pic R` which is
51	`Shrink (Skeleton (SemimoduleCat R))ˣ`. -/
52	def LineBundle (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type _ :=
53	  (Skeleton X.Modules)ˣ
54	
55	/-- Tensor product makes the type of line bundles a commutative group: the unit
56	is the structure sheaf `O_X`, multiplication is `⊗_{O_X}`, and the inverse of
57	a line bundle `L` is its dual `L^∨ = Hom_{O_X}(L, O_X)`.
58	
59	Derives via Lean's typeclass resolution from `BraidedCategory (X.Modules)`
60	through `Skeleton.instCommMonoid` and `instCommGroupUnits`. The
61	`BraidedCategory (X.Modules)` instance is provided by
62	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.instBraidedCategory` in
63	`Modules/Monoidal.lean`, transitively depending on `instIsMonoidal_W`. -/
64	noncomputable instance instCommGroupLineBundle (X : Scheme.{u}) :
65	    CommGroup (LineBundle X) :=
66	  inferInstanceAs (CommGroup (Skeleton X.Modules)ˣ)
67	
68	/-- The Picard group of a scheme: line bundles up to isomorphism. -/
69	abbrev Pic (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type _ := LineBundle X
70	
71	/-- **Mathlib gap (post-C1, named-deferred per Phase C1 default option (c))**.
72	The categorical pull-back functor `Scheme.Modules.pullback f : Y.Modules ⥤ X.Modules`
73	should be monoidal: `(pullback f).obj (M ⊗ N) ≅ (pullback f).obj M ⊗ (pullback f).obj N`
74	for every `M N : Y.Modules`. Mathlib b80f227 does not provide a
75	`Functor.Monoidal (Scheme.Modules.pullback f)` instance, so this iso must be
76	hand-built or named-deferred. The project takes the named-deferral route per
77	analogist `c1-route` (`analogies/c1-route.md`).
78	
79	A future Mathlib refresh that lands `(SheafOfModules.pullback _).Monoidal`
80	collapses this sorry to `MonoidalCategoryStruct.tensorObj_iso` (or the
81	canonical successor lemma). -/
82	noncomputable def SheafOfModules.pullback_tensorObj {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y)
83	    (M N : Y.Modules) :
84	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj (M ⊗ N) ≅
85	      (Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj M ⊗ (Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj N := by
86	  sorry
87	
88	/-- Pull-back of line bundles along a scheme morphism, as a group
89	homomorphism. Eventual implementation is
90	`Units.map (Skeleton.monoidHom (Scheme.Modules.pullback f))` once
91	`Scheme.Modules.pullback f` carries a `Functor.Monoidal` instance — currently a
92	Mathlib gap surfaced as `SheafOfModules.pullback_tensorObj` above. -/
93	noncomputable def Pic.pullback {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) :
94	    Pic Y →* Pic X := by
95	  sorry
96	
97	/-- Pull-back of line bundles along the identity is the identity homomorphism. -/
98	@[simp]
99	lemma Pic.pullback_id (X : Scheme.{u}) : Pic.pullback (𝟙 X) = MonoidHom.id (Pic X) := by
100	  sorry
101	
102	/-- Pull-back of line bundles is contravariantly functorial:
103	`(f ≫ g)^* = f^* ∘ g^*` on Picard groups. -/
104	@[simp]
105	lemma Pic.pullback_comp {X Y Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) (g : Y ⟶ Z) :
106	    Pic.pullback (f ≫ g) = (Pic.pullback f).comp (Pic.pullback g) := by
107	  sorry
108	
109	end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.LineBundle
7	
8	/-!
9	# The relative Picard functor
10	
11	Phase C step 2 (per `STRATEGY.md`): the relative Picard functor of a curve
12	`C` over `Spec k`, packaged as a contravariant functor on schemes-over-`k`,
13	plus the (deferred) representability theorem whose closure jointly unblocks
14	the four `Jacobian.lean` sorries.
15	
16	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_Functor.tex`.
17	
18	## Status (iteration 005 — first prover round)
19	
20	`PicardFunctor` is **filled**: for `C : Over (Spec k)` the functor sends each
21	`S : Over (Spec k)` to the cokernel-as-quotient
22	`Pic(C ×_k S) / p_S^* Pic(S)` of the pull-back homomorphism along the
23	projection `p_S : C ×_k S → S`. Functoriality in `S` is via the universal map
24	of fiber products and `Pic.pullback`.
25	
26	`PicardFunctor.representable` is intentionally left as `sorry` — FGA-level
27	result, not honestly closeable within this project's iteration scope.
28	
29	## Source category
30	
31	The directive's sketch had source `Schemeᵒᵖ`, but a generic `S : Scheme` does
32	not come equipped with a structure morphism to `Spec k` — without it the fiber
33	product `C ×_k S` is not canonically defined. Following the blueprint's
34	mathematical setup (`Sch_k = Sch / Spec k`) and the convention already used in
35	`Jacobian.lean`, we therefore take the source as `(Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of
36	k)))ᵒᵖ`, i.e.\ contravariant on `k`-schemes. This preserves the intended
37	functor while making the fiber product canonically defined; the
38	`IsRepresentable` predicate then asks for a representing object inside
39	`Over (Spec k)`, matching the geometric meaning of the Picard scheme as a
40	`k`-scheme. See `task_results/Picard_Functor.lean.md` for the rationale.
41	-/
42	
43	set_option autoImplicit false
44	
45	universe u
46	
47	open CategoryTheory Limits Opposite
48	
49	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
50	
51	variable {k : Type u} [Field k]
52	
53	namespace PicardFunctor
54	
55	/-- The map on fiber products `C ×_k S₁ → C ×_k S₂` induced by
56	`f : S₁ ⟶ S₂` in `Over (Spec k)`, given by the universal property of pullback
57	applied to `(𝟙 C, f.left)`. -/
58	noncomputable def fiberMap (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
59	    {S₁ S₂ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₁ ⟶ S₂) :
60	    Limits.pullback C.hom S₁.hom ⟶ Limits.pullback C.hom S₂.hom :=
61	  Limits.pullback.map _ _ _ _ (𝟙 _) f.left (𝟙 _) (by simp)
62	    (by rw [Category.comp_id]; exact (Over.w f).symm)
63	
64	@[simp, reassoc]
65	lemma fiberMap_comp_snd (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
66	    {S₁ S₂ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₁ ⟶ S₂) :
67	    fiberMap C f ≫ Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S₂.hom =
68	      Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S₁.hom ≫ f.left := by
69	  unfold fiberMap; exact Limits.pullback.lift_snd _ _ _
70	
71	@[simp, reassoc]
72	lemma fiberMap_comp_fst (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
73	    {S₁ S₂ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₁ ⟶ S₂) :
74	    fiberMap C f ≫ Limits.pullback.fst C.hom S₂.hom =
75	      Limits.pullback.fst C.hom S₁.hom := by
76	  unfold fiberMap; rw [Limits.pullback.lift_fst]; simp
77	
78	@[simp]
79	lemma fiberMap_id (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
80	    (S : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
81	    fiberMap C (𝟙 S) = 𝟙 _ := by
82	  unfold fiberMap; exact Limits.pullback.map_id
83	
84	lemma fiberMap_comp (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
85	    {S₁ S₂ S₃ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₁ ⟶ S₂) (g : S₂ ⟶ S₃) :
86	    fiberMap C (f ≫ g) = fiberMap C f ≫ fiberMap C g := by
87	  refine Limits.pullback.hom_ext ?_ ?_
88	  · simp
89	  · simp only [fiberMap_comp_snd, Category.assoc, Over.comp_left, fiberMap_comp_snd_assoc]
90	
91	/-- The pull-back homomorphism on the relative Picard quotient
92	`Pic(C ×_k S₁) / p_{S₁}^* Pic(S₁) → Pic(C ×_k S₂) / p_{S₂}^* Pic(S₂)` induced
93	by `f : S₂ ⟶ S₁` in `Over (Spec k)`.
94	
95	The descent through the quotient uses the commutative square
96	`fiberMap C f ≫ pullback.snd = pullback.snd ≫ f.left`
97	(`fiberMap_comp_snd`), which sends `p_{S₁}^* Pic(S₁)` into `p_{S₂}^* Pic(S₂)`
98	via `Pic.pullback (fiberMap C f)`. -/
99	noncomputable def quotMap (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
100	    {S₁ S₂ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₂ ⟶ S₁) :
101	    (Pic (Limits.pullback C.hom S₁.hom) ⧸
102	        (Pic.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S₁.hom)).range) →*
103	      (Pic (Limits.pullback C.hom S₂.hom) ⧸
104	        (Pic.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S₂.hom)).range) :=
105	  QuotientGroup.lift _
106	    ((QuotientGroup.mk' _).comp (Pic.pullback (fiberMap C f)))
107	    (by
108	      rintro x ⟨m, rfl⟩
109	      simp only [MonoidHom.mem_ker, MonoidHom.coe_comp, Function.comp_apply,
110	        QuotientGroup.mk'_apply]
111	      rw [QuotientGroup.eq_one_iff]
112	      refine ⟨Pic.pullback f.left m, ?_⟩
113	      rw [← MonoidHom.comp_apply (Pic.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S₂.hom)),
114	          ← Pic.pullback_comp, ← fiberMap_comp_snd, Pic.pullback_comp,
115	          MonoidHom.comp_apply])
116	
117	@[simp]
118	lemma quotMap_mk (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
119	    {S₁ S₂ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₂ ⟶ S₁)
120	    (x : Pic (Limits.pullback C.hom S₁.hom)) :
121	    quotMap C f (x : _ ⧸ _) = ((Pic.pullback (fiberMap C f) x) : _ ⧸ _) := rfl
122	
123	@[simp]
124	lemma quotMap_id (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
125	    (S : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
126	    quotMap C (𝟙 S) = MonoidHom.id _ := by
127	  ext x; simp
128	
129	lemma quotMap_comp (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
130	    {S₁ S₂ S₃ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₂ ⟶ S₁) (g : S₃ ⟶ S₂) :
131	    quotMap C (g ≫ f) = (quotMap C g).comp (quotMap C f) := by
132	  ext x
133	  simp [fiberMap_comp, Pic.pullback_comp]
134	
135	end PicardFunctor
136	
137	/-- The relative Picard functor of `C` over `Spec k`:
138	    `S ↦ Pic(C ×_k S) / p_S^* Pic(S)`,
139	where `p_S : C ×_k S → S` is the projection. Functoriality in `S` is via base
140	change of the fiber product (`PicardFunctor.fiberMap`) and the pull-back
141	homomorphism on Picard groups (`Pic.pullback`).
142	
143	**Iteration 005 implementation.** The fiber product is taken in
144	`Limits.pullback C.hom S.unop.hom`, the projection `p_S = pullback.snd`, and
145	the quotient by the pull-back range uses `QuotientGroup.lift` /
146	`QuotientGroup.mk'`. See `PicardFunctor.fiberMap` and `PicardFunctor.quotMap`
147	for the underlying constructions and the source-category note in the file
148	docstring. -/
149	noncomputable def PicardFunctor
150	    (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
151	    (Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type (u + 1) where
152	  obj S := Pic (Limits.pullback C.hom S.unop.hom) ⧸
153	    (Pic.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S.unop.hom)).range
154	  map {_ _} f := TypeCat.ofHom fun x ↦ PicardFunctor.quotMap C f.unop x
155	  map_id _ := by
156	    apply TypeCat.Hom.ext; apply TypeCat.Fun.ext
157	    funext x
158	    change PicardFunctor.quotMap C (𝟙 _) x = x
159	    simp
160	  map_comp f g := by
161	    apply TypeCat.Hom.ext; apply TypeCat.Fun.ext
162	    funext x
163	    change PicardFunctor.quotMap C (f ≫ g).unop x =
164	      PicardFunctor.quotMap C g.unop (PicardFunctor.quotMap C f.unop x)
165	    rw [unop_comp, PicardFunctor.quotMap_comp]; rfl
166	
167	/-- Representability of the relative Picard functor for a smooth, proper,
168	geometrically irreducible curve `C` over a field `k` (Grothendieck FGA, Mumford
169	*Abelian Varieties* §III.13). The connected component of the identity of the
170	representing group scheme is the Jacobian `Jac(C)`.
171	
172	**Intentionally deferred.** This is FGA-level and not honestly closeable on
173	the global-sections-approximate `LineBundle`. The four `Jacobian.lean` sorries
174	all reduce to this theorem. Do not attempt to fill it in iter-005 — see the
175	file docstring and `Picard_Functor.tex` forward-compatibility note. -/
176	theorem PicardFunctor.representable
177	    (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
178	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
179	    [IsProper C.hom]
180	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] :
181	    (PicardFunctor C).IsRepresentable := sorry
182	
183	end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
184

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.Functor
7	
8	/-!
9	# The relative Picard functor as an abelian-group-valued presheaf
10	
11	Phase C step 3 setup (per `STRATEGY.md`): the `AddCommGrpCat`-valued variant
12	of the relative Picard functor of Chapter `Picard_Functor.tex`. The
13	underlying-set functor agrees on the nose with the iter-004 `PicardFunctor`;
14	only the codomain changes from `Type u` to `AddCommGrpCat.{u}`. This is the
15	input shape required by `CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf` for the (deferred)
16	étale sheafification of the Picard presheaf.
17	
18	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FunctorAb.tex`.
19	
20	## Status (iteration 004 — prover round)
21	
22	`PicardFunctorAb` is **filled**: the underlying-set quotient
23	`Pic(C ×_k S) / p_S^* Pic(S)` is wrapped through `Additive` (which converts
24	the multiplicative `CommGroup` instance on `Pic` to the `AddCommGroup`
25	instance demanded by `AddCommGrpCat.of`), and the action on morphisms is
26	`PicardFunctor.quotMap` post-composed with `MonoidHom.toAdditive` and bundled
27	via `AddCommGrpCat.ofHom`. Functoriality reduces on the nose to
28	`PicardFunctor.quotMap_id` / `PicardFunctor.quotMap_comp` and the
29	multiplicative-to-additive equiv `MonoidHom.toAdditive`.
30	
31	## Status (iteration 008 — étale-sheafification scaffold)
32	
33	`PicardFunctorAb.etaleSheafified` is **scaffolded**: the étale-topology
34	sheafification of `PicardFunctorAb`, packaged as a sheaf on
35	`Scheme.etaleTopology.{u}` over `Spec k` valued in `AddCommGrpCat.{u+1}`.
36	The body post-composes with `AddCommGrpCat.uliftFunctor.{u+1, u}` to bridge
37	the universe gap between the iter-004 codomain `AddCommGrpCat.{u}` and the
38	value-universe `AddCommGrpCat.{u+1}` at which `HasSheafify` is inferable in
39	current Mathlib. The closure body is a one-liner against
40	`CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf`; the prover round will install it.
41	-/
42	
43	set_option autoImplicit false
44	
45	universe u
46	
47	open CategoryTheory Limits Opposite
48	
49	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
50	
51	variable {k : Type u} [Field k]
52	
53	/-- Phase C step 3 codomain change: the `AddCommGrpCat`-valued variant of the
54	relative Picard functor. The underlying-set functor agrees on the nose with
55	`PicardFunctor` (Chapter `Picard_Functor.tex`); only the codomain changes.
56	Wraps `PicardFunctor.quotMap` via `AddCommGrpCat.ofHom`, transporting the
57	multiplicative `Pic` quotient through `Additive` to obtain the
58	`AddCommGroup` instance required by `AddCommGrpCat.of`. -/
59	noncomputable def PicardFunctorAb
60	    (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
61	    (Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))ᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrpCat.{u+1} where
62	  obj S := AddCommGrpCat.of (Additive
63	    (Pic (Limits.pullback C.hom S.unop.hom) ⧸
64	      (Pic.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S.unop.hom)).range))
65	  map {_ _} f :=
66	    AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (MonoidHom.toAdditive (PicardFunctor.quotMap C f.unop))
67	  map_id _ := by
68	    simp only [unop_id, PicardFunctor.quotMap_id, MonoidHom.toAdditive_id,
69	      AddCommGrpCat.ofHom_id]
70	  map_comp f g := by
71	    simp only [unop_comp, PicardFunctor.quotMap_comp]
72	    rw [show MonoidHom.toAdditive
73	        ((PicardFunctor.quotMap C g.unop).comp (PicardFunctor.quotMap C f.unop)) =
74	        (MonoidHom.toAdditive (PicardFunctor.quotMap C g.unop)).comp
75	          (MonoidHom.toAdditive (PicardFunctor.quotMap C f.unop)) from rfl,
76	      AddCommGrpCat.ofHom_comp]
77	
78	/-- Phase C step 3 forget-and-recover: the natural iso recovering
79	`PicardFunctor C` from `PicardFunctorAb C ⋙ forget AddCommGrpCat`. The
80	underlying-set functor of `PicardFunctorAb` agrees on the nose with
81	`PicardFunctor` (via the type-equal `Additive` wrapper of the iter-004
82	closure), so the iso is the identity natural transformation. See blueprint
83	chapter `Picard_FunctorAb.tex` (`def:PicardFunctorAb_forgetCompare`). -/
84	noncomputable def PicardFunctorAb.forgetCompare
85	    (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
86	    PicardFunctorAb C ⋙ CategoryTheory.forget AddCommGrpCat.{u+1} ≅ PicardFunctor C :=
87	  NatIso.ofComponents (fun _ => Iso.refl _) (by intros; rfl)
88	
89	/-- Object-evaluation simp lemma for `PicardFunctorAb` along the forgetful
90	functor. The `Additive` wrapper inside the iter-004 `PicardFunctorAb` is
91	type-equal to its underlying multiplicative quotient, so applying
92	`forget AddCommGrpCat` recovers `(PicardFunctor C).obj S` on the nose. Tagged
93	`@[simp]` for downstream rewriting. Phase C step 4 polish (iter-007). -/
94	@[simp] lemma PicardFunctorAb_forget_obj (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
95	    (S : (Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))ᵒᵖ) :
96	    (CategoryTheory.forget AddCommGrpCat).obj ((PicardFunctorAb C).obj S)
97	      = (PicardFunctor C).obj S :=
98	  rfl
99	
100	/-- Phase C step 3 *proper*: étale-sheafify `PicardFunctorAb C` to obtain
101	a sheaf on `Scheme.etaleTopology.{u}` over `Spec k` valued in
102	`AddCommGrpCat.{u+1}`. Post-C1 the codomain of `PicardFunctorAb` is already
103	`AddCommGrpCat.{u+1}` (the universe bumped to match the new `Type (u+1)`
104	codomain of `PicardFunctor`), so the universe-lift wrapper used pre-C1 is
105	no longer needed. See blueprint chapter `Picard_FunctorAb.tex`
106	(`def:PicardFunctorAb_etaleSheafified`). -/
107	noncomputable def PicardFunctorAb.etaleSheafified
108	    (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
109	    Sheaf ((Scheme.etaleTopology.{u}).over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
110	      AddCommGrpCat.{u+1} :=
111	  (CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf _ _).obj (PicardFunctorAb C)
112	
113	end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme

12	scheme as the units of a monoidal category of `O_X`-modules**, and is that
13	idiom executable today against the project's already-shipped
14	`MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` / `BraidedCategory (X.Modules)` chain
15	(which transitively depends on the deferred `instIsMonoidal_W` sorry for
16	`(W X).IsMonoidal`)?

19	
20	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundle.lean:85-122` — current
21	  `LineBundle X := CommRing.Pic Γ(X, ⊤)` and `Pic.pullback` via
22	  `CommRing.Pic.mapRingHom`.

28	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FunctorAb.lean` — additive-group flavor
29	  of the same construction.

31	## Decisions identified
32	
33	The directive's single design question compresses four orthogonal decisions.
34	
35	### Decision A: name of the "invertible-objects" type
36	
37	- **Mathlib idiom**: there is **no type literally named
38	  `MonoidalCategory.Invertible`** in Mathlib b80f227. The canonical idiom is
39	  `(Skeleton C)ˣ` — the **units of the (skeleton's) monoid structure on the
40	  objects of a (braided) monoidal category**. Specifically:
41	  - `CategoryTheory.Skeleton.instMonoid` (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Skeleton:60`)
42	    promotes `Skeleton C` to `Monoid` from `[MonoidalCategory C]`.
43	  - `CategoryTheory.Skeleton.instCommMonoid` (`...:80`) promotes it to
44	    `CommMonoid` from `[BraidedCategory C]`.
45	  - `(Skeleton C)ˣ` is then automatically `CommGroup` from `instCommGroupUnits`.
46	  - The ring precedent (`CommRing.Pic`,
47	    `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup:407-408`) defines
48	    `def CommRing.Pic (R) := Shrink (Skeleton (SemimoduleCat R))ˣ`.
49	
50	  Why Mathlib chose this: skeleton-level units neatly factor the
51	  *non-categorical* equational content (groupy laws on iso-classes) from
52	  the *categorical* content (associator/unitor/braiding witnesses). The
53	  `Shrink` wrapper handles universe issues (`SemimoduleCat R`'s skeleton
54	  lives in `u+1` a priori; Mathlib proves it is `Small.{u}` via the
55	  finite-presentation classifier).
56	
57	- **Project's proposed path** (`STRATEGY.md` "Phase C1" §): refactor
58	  `LineBundle X` to "`MonoidalCategory.Invertible (X.Modules)`". This name
59	  **does not denote any Mathlib declaration**; it is a placeholder that
60	  must be resolved to the actual idiom.
61	
62	- **Gap**: divergent-and-wrong (a literal `MonoidalCategory.Invertible`
63	  does not exist; the strategy text needs to commit to the correct name).
64	  Easy to fix at refactor-spec time.
65	
66	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB**. Refactor target should be
67	  `(Skeleton X.Modules)ˣ` (or `Shrink (Skeleton X.Modules)ˣ` if a
68	  universe-size issue arises), mirroring `CommRing.Pic`.
69	
70	### Decision B: dependency on the deferred `instIsMonoidal_W` sorry
71	
72	- **Mathlib idiom**: `(Skeleton C)ˣ` as a `CommGroup` requires
73	  `[BraidedCategory C]` (to get `CommMonoid (Skeleton C)`).
74	  - For the project, `BraidedCategory (X.Modules)` is obtained transitively
75	    via `Localization.Monoidal.Braided` (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Braided:118-123`,
76	    `instance : BraidedCategory (LocalizedMonoidal L W ε)`), which assumes
77	    `[W.IsMonoidal]` and `[BraidedCategory C₀]` (in fact, the project's

79	    `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal:145`).

89	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost (vs. the strategy text framing

91	  trigger claims the project's terminal sorry-set is 3 (or 4 with L1802)

130	### Decision C: pull-back functoriality on `LineBundle`
131	
132	- **Mathlib idiom (analogous to `CommRing.Pic.mapRingHom`)**: a monoidal
133	  functor `F : C ⥤ D` induces a monoid homomorphism `Skeleton C →* Skeleton D`
134	  via `Skeleton.monoidHom` (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Skeleton:111`),
135	  which restricts to `(Skeleton C)ˣ →* (Skeleton D)ˣ` via `Units.map`.
136	  - The categorical pull-back functor for sheaves of modules is
137	    `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.pullback f : Y.Modules ⥤ X.Modules`
138	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf:167-168`,
139	    delegating to `SheafOfModules.pullback`).
140	  - To use `Skeleton.monoidHom`, we need a `Functor.Monoidal` instance on

142	
143	- **Mathlib status of this instance**: **MISSING**. A search across
144	  `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.*`,
145	  `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.*`, and
146	  `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.*` finds no

163	- **Gap**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL. **This is the largest hidden cost**

169	  sheafifications presents differently from the localization picture
170	  — Mathlib's `SheafOfModules.pullback` is *not* a localization functor).
171	
172	- **Verdict**: **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL**, with a recommended detour:
173	  define `Pic.pullback` directly via `pullback.obj` on representatives,
174	  manually witnessing `pullback (M ⊗ N) ≅ pullback M ⊗ pullback N` as
175	  the proof obligation. Equivalently, the C1 refactor should produce
176	  this iso as a stand-alone lemma `SheafOfModules.pullback_tensorObj`
177	  (or accept a *second* named deferred sorry as the price of the
178	  refactor).
179	
180	### Decision D: how to register an inhabitant of `LineBundle X`
181	
182	- **Mathlib idiom**: `CommRing.Pic.mk : Type → Pic R`
183	  (`Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup:437-441`) takes a `Module R M`
184	  with a `Module.Invertible R M` typeclass and produces
185	  `Pic R = Shrink (Skeleton (SemimoduleCat R))ˣ` via
186	  `Units.mkOfMulEqOne ⟦.of R M'⟧ ⟦.of R (Dual R M')⟧` plus
187	  a soundness proof.
188	  - The typeclass `Module.Invertible R M` (`...:78`) is defined as
189	    "the canonical map `Mᵛ ⊗[R] M → R` is bijective". This requires the

192	- **Project-side analog requirement**: to register an `M : X.Modules`
193	  as an element of `LineBundle X = (Skeleton X.Modules)ˣ`, the project
194	  must provide a "scheme-side invertibility" witness. Mathlib has no
195	  `SheafOfModules.Invertible` typeclass; the most direct route is to

197	  `M ⊗ M⁻¹ ≅ 𝟙_` in `X.Modules`.
198	
199	- **Gap**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL or PROCEED-by-construction.
200	  - On affine `X = Spec R`: a Mathlib-aligned bridge would derive
201	    `Module.Invertible R M ⇒ Sheaf-side invertibility of `M~``, but the

204	    `Functor.Monoidal` instance in Mathlib b80f227 (and `Tilde` is at

\subsection{Forward-compatibility note (LineBundle approximation)}

Chapter~\ref{chap:Picard_LineBundle} defines line bundles via a global-sections approximation, correct on affine schemes but a strict subgroup of the true $\Pic(X)$ on non-affine schemes. The \emph{definition} of $\PicardFunctor C$ therefore inherits this approximation: the values $\PicardFunctor(S)$ for non-affine $S$ are smaller subgroups of the true relative Picard group. Consequently the representability theorem stated above will be honest only after the line bundle definition is refined to the bespoke ``invertible quasi-coherent sheaf'' definition (gated on monoidal-category infrastructure for $\mathcal O_X$-modules, currently absent). Until that refinement lands, the representable sorry of this chapter must remain unattacked: closing it on the approximate definition would silently assert representability of the wrong functor.
